segments.inferno-6-g1: “Al tornar” is a contracted prepositional infinitive, literally “at the returning,” used temporally: “when ... returned.” “De la mente” depends on “tornar,” giving the thing that returns, namely Dante’s mind or consciousness.
segments.inferno-6-g2: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “la mente.” “Si chiuse” is past tense with reflexive “si,” meaning the mind “closed itself”; “dinanzi a” means “before/in the face of,” and “d’i” is the contraction of “di i,” “of the.”
segments.inferno-6-g3: This second “che” most naturally refers to “la pietà”: the pity was what overwhelmed him. “Di trestizia” expresses the cause or coloring of the action, “with/by sadness,” while “tutto” is adverbial, “completely”; “confuse” is a past-tense poetic form for “confounded/overwhelmed.”
segments.inferno-6-g4: The direct objects, “novi tormenti e novi tormentati,” are placed before the verb for emphasis. “Tormentati” is a substantivized adjective, “the tormented ones”; “mi veggio intorno” means “I see around me,” with “mi” marking Dante’s own surrounding space.
segments.inferno-6-g5: “Come ch’io ...” introduces an indefinite/concessive clause: “however I may ...” or “whichever way I ... .” The verbs “mova” and “volga” are present subjunctives governed by this construction, and “mi” is reflexive with both: “move myself” and “turn myself.”
segments.inferno-6-g6: This continues the same “come che” pattern from the previous segment. “Guati” is present subjunctive, “I may look/gaze,” completing the series: no matter how he moves, turns, or looks, he sees the torments around him.
segments.inferno-6-g7: “Io sono” is present tense, here meaning “I am located.” “Al terzo cerchio” uses “a + il” in the sense of being at or in a place, and “cerchio” refers to a circle of Hell.
segments.inferno-6-g8: “De la piova etterna” specifies the defining feature of the third circle: it is the circle “of the eternal rain.” “Piova” is an archaic/poetic noun for “rain,” and “etterna” is a poetic form of “eterna.”
segments.inferno-6-g9: “Maladetta” is an adjective agreeing with “piova,” continuing the description of the rain. It is an archaic form equivalent to “maledetta,” “cursed” or “accursed.”
segments.inferno-6-g10: “Fredda e greve” adds two more adjectives, again agreeing with the feminine noun “piova.” “Greve” means physically heavy and oppressive, fitting the punishing rain.
segments.inferno-6-g11: “Regola e qualità” are the subject: the rain’s pattern and nature. “Mai non” is a medieval emphatic negative meaning “never,” and “l’è” contains a dative pronoun referring back to the rain, “to it”; “nova” means that no change is ever new to this rain because it is always the same.
segments.inferno-6-g12: “Grandine grossa” begins the compound subject of the sentence. The adjective “grossa” agrees with the feminine noun “grandine” and means large or heavy hail.
segments.inferno-6-g13: “Acqua tinta e neve” continues the compound subject begun by “Grandine grossa.” “Per l’aere tenebroso” means “through the dark air,” and “si riversa” is a reflexive/intransitive construction, literally “pours itself down,” with the singular verb treating the mixed precipitation as a mass.
segments.inferno-6-g14: “Pute” is an archaic present-tense form meaning “stinks.” “Che” introduces a relative clause modifying “la terra,” and “questo” sums up the foul mixture of hail, dirty water, and snow that the ground receives.
segments.inferno-6-g15: “Cerbero” is the subject of the sentence. Dante names the mythological guardian of the underworld before describing him in apposition.
segments.inferno-6-g16: “Fiera crudele e diversa” is an appositive phrase describing Cerberus: “a cruel and strange beast.” “Diversa” here does not merely mean “different,” but “strange” or monstrous in appearance.
segments.inferno-6-g17: “Con tre gole” gives the means or bodily feature by which Cerberus barks: “with three throats.” “Latra” is present tense, “barks,” while “sovra la gente” means “over the people”; the relative clause “che quivi è sommersa” uses “è” as a passive auxiliary, “who are submerged there.”
segments.inferno-6-g18: The object-like noun phrase “Li occhi” is placed before the verb for emphasis: literally “the eyes he has vermilion.” “Li” is an archaic masculine plural article, and “ha” is present indicative of “avere.”
segments.inferno-6-g19: This segment continues the same elliptical construction governed by “ha”: “he has the beard greasy and black.” The verb is omitted because it is understood from the previous segment; “unta” and “atra” are feminine singular adjectives agreeing with “barba.”
segments.inferno-6-g20: The conjunction “e” adds another body part to the list, again with “ha” understood. “’l” is the contracted form of “il,” and “largo” agrees with masculine singular “ventre.”
segments.inferno-6-g21: This is another elliptical item in the descriptive series: “and the hands clawed.” “Unghiate” is a feminine plural adjective agreeing with “le mani,” which is feminine plural in Italian though it refers to hands.
segments.inferno-6-g22: The subject is still Cerberus, and the three present-tense verbs give a rapid sequence of violent actions. “Li spirti” uses archaic “li” for modern “gli/i,” while “iscoia” and “isquatra” have prosthetic initial i-, a common old form before s + consonant.
segments.inferno-6-g23: “Urlar” is the apocopated infinitive “urlare,” used with the causative verb “fa”: literally “the rain makes them howl.” The pronoun “li” is the direct object, “them,” and the subject “la pioggia” follows the verb phrase in poetic word order.
segments.inferno-6-g24: “De l’un de’ lati” means “of/from one of the sides,” with “de’” contracted from “de li/dei.” The phrase “fanno ... schermo” means “make a shield/shelter,” and “a l’altro” gives the beneficiary or direction: for the other side.
segments.inferno-6-g25: “Volgonsi” is an enclitic form of “si volgono,” meaning “they turn themselves” or “turn over.” The verb comes before its subject “i miseri profani,” a normal poetic inversion, while “spesso” modifies the repeated action.
segments.inferno-6-g26: “Quando” introduces a temporal subordinate clause. “Ci” is the direct object “us,” placed before “scorse,” a past-tense form meaning “saw” or “caught sight of”; the subject “Cerbero” follows the verb.
segments.inferno-6-g27: “Il gran vermo” is an appositive renaming Cerberus with a degrading monstrous image. “Gran” is the shortened form of “grande,” used before the noun.
segments.inferno-6-g28: The main verbs are “aperse” and “mostrocci”: “he opened” and “he showed us.” “Mostrocci” is not a noun here but an old enclitic form, “mostrò + ci,” with the object pronoun attached after the verb; “le sanne” means the fangs.
segments.inferno-6-g29: “Avea” is an archaic or poetic imperfect form of “aveva,” “he had.” The relative clause “che tenesse fermo” uses the imperfect subjunctive “tenesse” after a negative indefinite expression, giving the sense “not a limb that he kept still.”
segments.inferno-6-g30: “’l” is the contracted article “il,” and “duca mio” means “my guide,” with the possessive following the noun in normal Italian fashion. “Distese” is a preterite form, “stretched out,” and “spanne” literally suggests outspread handspans or open hands.
segments.inferno-6-g31: “Prese” is another preterite verb coordinated with “distese,” continuing the sequence of actions. “La terra” is the direct object, meaning the earth or dirt he picks up.
segments.inferno-6-g32: “Con piene le pugna” is an archaic construction meaning “with the fists full”; “pugna” here is an old plural for fists, not “battles.” “La” before “gittò” is a direct-object pronoun referring back to “terra,” and “dentro a le” means “into the,” with “canne” poetically meaning throats or gullets.
segments.inferno-6-g67: The clause begins with coordinating E, linking Ciacco’s statement to what he has just said. Io anima trista is appositional—“I, a wretched soul”—and son is an abbreviated poetic form of sono, first-person singular present of essere; sola agrees with the speaker understood as anima, hence feminine singular.
segments.inferno-6-g68: Ché introduces the reason for the previous statement: “because/for.” Tutte queste refers to the other souls nearby; stanno is present indicative and here means “are/remain” in a condition, with a simil pena indicating the punishment they undergo and per simil colpa giving the cause, “for a similar sin.”
segments.inferno-6-g69: E links this narrative comment to Ciacco’s speech. Più non is the normal Italian negative sequence meaning “no longer/no more,” and fé is a poetic contracted preterite of fece, “made/did”; far parola literally means “make a word,” idiomatically “speak.”
segments.inferno-6-g70: Io is an explicit subject pronoun, used for emphasis before the verb. Li is an archaic dative pronoun equivalent to modern gli, “to him,” and rispuosi is the first-person singular passato remoto of rispondere, here “I answered.”
segments.inferno-6-g71: Ciacco is a direct vocative: Dante addresses the soul by the name he gave for himself. It stands outside the syntax of the following clauses.
segments.inferno-6-g72: Il tuo affanno is the subject of pesa, while mi is an indirect object meaning “on me/to me.” Pesa is present indicative and is used figuratively, “weighs heavily” or “burdens”; sì means “so,” preparing the result clause introduced by ch’ in the next segment.
segments.inferno-6-g73: Ch’ is elided che and introduces a consecutive result clause after sì: “so much that….” A lagrimar uses a plus an infinitive, “to weep,” with the poetic truncated infinitive lagrimar for lagrimare; mi ’nvita is mi invita with initial i elided after the pronoun, “invites/moves me.”
segments.inferno-6-g74: Ma turns from Dante’s pity to his request. Dimmi is an imperative form di’ plus the enclitic pronoun mi, “tell me.”
segments.inferno-6-g75: Se tu sai is a parenthetical condition, “if you know.” Tu is explicit for clarity and emphasis, and sai is present indicative, not subjunctive, because Dante states the condition plainly.
segments.inferno-6-g76: A che verranno literally means “to what they will come,” hence “what will become of them” or “what outcome they will reach.” Verranno is future indicative; li cittadin is an archaic plural with li as article and cittadin as a truncated plural form, while de la città partita means “of the divided city,” referring to Florence split by factions.
segments.inferno-6-g77: S’ is elided se, introducing another indirect question dependent on dimmi: “whether….” Alcun is singular and means “anyone,” v’ is vi meaning “there,” and è is present indicative of essere, so the phrase asks whether any righteous person exists there.
segments.inferno-6-g78: E dimmi coordinates a third request with the earlier dimmi. La cagione is “the reason,” followed by per che, an older spelling of perché/why; l’ refers back to la città, while ha assalita is the passato prossimo with avere, and assalita agrees in form with the feminine object pronoun in this poetic construction.
segments.inferno-6-g79: E links the reply to Dante’s question. Quelli, literally “that one,” is a common narrative pronoun for the person just mentioned, and a me marks the indirect object: “to me.”
segments.inferno-6-g80: Dopo lunga tencione is a temporal phrase, “after long strife,” with tencione an archaic/poetic noun for contention. Verranno is future indicative; al sangue, literally “to the blood,” idiomatically means “to bloodshed” or violent conflict.
segments.inferno-6-g81: E coordinates the second future event with verranno. La parte selvaggia is the subject, “the savage/wild party,” caccerà is future indicative meaning “will drive out,” and l’altra is the object, “the other party”; con molta offensione adds the manner or result, “with much injury/harm.”
segments.inferno-6-g82: “Poi appresso” is a reinforced temporal phrase, meaning “then afterward”; “appresso” here is adverbial, not a finite verb. “Convien che” means “it is necessary that” and takes the subjunctive “caggia,” an archaic form of “cada” from “cadere”; “questa” refers to the party just mentioned. “Infra tre soli” literally means “within three suns,” i.e. within three solar cycles, commonly understood as three years.
segments.inferno-6-g83: The second “che” is coordinated with the first after “convien”: it is necessary both that this party fall and that the other “sormonti,” a present subjunctive meaning “rise above” or “gain the upper hand.” “Con la forza di tal” means “by/with the power of such a one,” where “tal” is an indefinite reference to a certain person. “Che testé piaggia” is a relative clause: “who is now maneuvering/temporizing,” with “testé” meaning “just now, at present.”
segments.inferno-6-g84: “Alte” is placed first for emphasis and agrees with “le fronti”: literally, “it will keep the foreheads high.” “Terrà” is the future of “tenere,” here meaning “will keep/hold.” The idiom expresses proud dominance: the victorious party will hold its head high for a long time.
segments.inferno-6-g85: “Tenendo” is a gerund that explains the manner or accompanying action of the previous clause: it keeps itself exalted by keeping the other side down. “L’altra” means “the other party,” and “sotto gravi pesi” is both literal and figurative: under heavy, burdensome weights or oppression.
segments.inferno-6-g86: “Come che” is concessive: “however much” or “although.” “Pianga” is present subjunctive, matching the concessive force; the subject is the oppressed “other” party. “N’aonti” contracts “ne” plus a subjunctive verb from an archaic verb meaning “to be shamed/disgraced by it,” but the supplied glosses for “aonti” do not match this form.
segments.inferno-6-g87: “Giusti” is substantival: “righteous people” or “just men.” “Son” is the shortened poetic form of “sono,” and the word order puts “Giusti” first for emphasis: “The just are two,” i.e. there are only two righteous people.
segments.inferno-6-g88: “Vi” means “there,” referring to the city or community under discussion. “Sono intesi” is a passive-like construction with the past participle of “intendere”; here it means “are listened to/heeded” rather than simply “are understood.” The negative “non” states that those righteous people receive no attention there.
segments.inferno-6-g89: “Superbia” begins a three-part subject completed in the next segment. It is placed alone and prominently, naming the first vice in the list of causes.
segments.inferno-6-g90: “Invidia e avarizia” complete the compound subject begun by “superbia.” “Sono le tre faville” identifies these vices as “the three sparks”; “c’hanno” is elided for “che hanno,” where “che” is a relative pronoun meaning “that/which.” “I cuori accesi” means “hearts kindled/inflamed,” with “accesi” functioning as a past participle/adjective after “hanno.”
segments.inferno-6-g91: “Qui” means “here” in the narrative sequence, that is, “at this point.” “Puose” is an archaic preterite form of “pose/put” from “porre,” and “puose fine a” means “put an end to.” “Al lagrimabil suono” means “to the lamentable/tearful sound,” referring to the speaker’s sorrowful utterance.
segments.inferno-6-g33: “Qual è” opens a comparative construction: literally “what/as is that dog,” answered later by “cotai” (“such/so”) in the main clause. “Ch’” is a relative pronoun referring to “cane,” and “abbaiando” is a gerund meaning “while barking.”
segments.inferno-6-g34: The subject is still “quel cane”; “si racqueta” is reflexive/intransitive, “calms itself down.” “Poi che” functions as a temporal conjunction, “after/once,” and “’l” is the contracted article “il.”
segments.inferno-6-g35: “Ché” gives the reason for the dog’s quieting: “because/for.” “A divorarlo” depends on “intende,” in the sense “attends to/is intent on,” while “pugna” is a finite verb here meaning “struggles,” coordinated with “intende.”
segments.inferno-6-g36: This is the main clause completing the simile begun with “Qual”: “cotai” means “such/so,” referring back to the dog’s behavior. “Si fecer” is an archaic contracted form of “si fecero,” literally “made themselves,” here meaning “became”; “de lo” is the older uncontracted form of modern “dello.”
segments.inferno-6-g37: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to “Cerbero.” “’Ntrona” is an elided form with initial vowel lost, meaning “deafens/dazes,” and “sì” is an intensifier, “so,” preparing the result clause that follows.
segments.inferno-6-g38: The second “ch’” introduces the result after “sì”: “so much that.” “Vorrebber” is an archaic third-person plural conditional, modern “vorrebbero,” and “esser sorde” is the infinitive complement, “to be deaf.”
segments.inferno-6-g39: “Passavam” is an archaic/imperfect first-person plural form, modern “passavamo,” describing ongoing movement. “Su per” together gives the sense of moving over/across the shades, and the relative clause “che adona la greve pioggia” has “la greve pioggia” as the subject: the heavy rain overwhelms or beats down the shades.
segments.inferno-6-g40: “Ponavam” is another imperfect first-person plural form, modern “ponevamo,” coordinated with “passavam.” “Le piante” means the soles of the feet, and “lor vanità” means the shades’ insubstantial emptiness; the relative “che” refers to that “vanità,” which only appears to be a real body/person.
segments.inferno-6-g41: “Elle” refers back to the feminine plural “anime” and means “they.” “Giacean” is an archaic imperfect plural form, modern “giacevano,” and “tutte quante” is an emphatic expression meaning “all of them.”
segments.inferno-6-g42: “Fuor d’una” means “except one,” with “fuor” used prepositionally. “Ch’” introduces a relative clause referring to that one soul, and “a seder si levò” means “rose to sit,” with “si levò” functioning reflexively/intransitively, “raised herself/rose.”
segments.inferno-6-g43: “Ratto ch’” is an idiomatic temporal expression meaning “as soon as.” “Ella ci vide passarsi davante” has “ci” as the object of “vide,” while “passarsi davante” means “pass before her,” with “si” attached to the infinitive in a dative/reflexive nuance.
segments.inferno-6-g44: This is a vocative address: “O tu” means “O you.” “Se’” is the apocopated form of “sei,” “you are,” and “tratto” is a passive past participle from “trarre,” here meaning “drawn/led,” though that sense is not among the listed options.
segments.inferno-6-g45: “Mi disse” is the reporting clause, literally “said to me.” The indirect object pronoun “mi” precedes the verb, as is normal in Italian.
segments.inferno-6-g46: “Riconoscimi” is an imperative with the object pronoun attached to the verb: “recognize me.” The command is addressed directly to Dante.
segments.inferno-6-g47: Here “se” is the conjunction “if,” not the elided verb “se’.” “Sai” is from “sapere” and in this context means “are able/can,” not merely “know.”
segments.inferno-6-g48: “Tu fosti” begins a past-tense passive construction whose participle is delayed until “fatto” at the end of the sentence. “Fosti” is the second-person singular preterite of “essere,” “you were,” but the listed senses do not include that value.
segments.inferno-6-g49: “Prima ch’” means “before,” introducing a subordinate temporal clause. “Io disfatto” is elliptical for “io fossi disfatto,” “I was undone/destroyed,” with the auxiliary omitted poetically.
segments.inferno-6-g50: “Fatto” completes “tu fosti ... fatto”: “you were made.” Dante separates auxiliary and participle around the subordinate clause for rhyme and emphasis, producing the contrast “made” versus “unmade.”
segments.inferno-6-g51: This is an elliptical speech tag: the verb of saying is omitted, as often in Dante, and must be understood as “I said.” “A lui” means “to him,” marking the addressee of Dante’s reply.
segments.inferno-6-g52: “L’angoscia che tu hai” is the subject of “tira”: literally, “the anguish that you have.” “Ti tira fuor de la mia mente” uses poetic word order with “ti” as the object: the anguish pulls “you” out of Dante’s mind or memory; “de la” is an older separated form of modern “della.”
segments.inferno-6-g53: “Sì che” introduces a result clause, “so that.” “Par” is impersonal, “it seems,” and “ch’i’” is the contraction of “che io”; “vedessi” is imperfect subjunctive after “par,” while “non ... mai” means “never” or “not ever.”
segments.inferno-6-g54: “Dimmi” is the imperative “tell” plus the enclitic pronoun “mi,” “tell me.” “Chi tu se’” means “who you are,” with “se’” as an archaic second-person singular form of “essere”; the following “che” resumes the person addressed, “who/that,” and “se’ messo” is a passive construction, “you are placed,” with “’n” understood before “sì dolente loco.”
segments.inferno-6-g55: “Hai ... pena” means “you have/undergo a punishment.” “Sì fatta” literally means “so made,” but idiomatically means “such” or “of such a kind,” with “fatta” agreeing in feminine singular with “pena.”
segments.inferno-6-g56: This “che” introduces the result of “sì fatta pena”: “such a punishment that...” It links the degree of the punishment to the comparison that follows.
segments.inferno-6-g57: “S’altra” is an elision of “se altra,” meaning “if another,” with “pena” understood from the previous segment. “Maggio” is a poetic shortened form of “maggiore,” “greater.”
segments.inferno-6-g58: “Nulla” functions as a negative pronoun meaning “none” or “nothing.” “È sì spiacente” means “is so displeasing/grievous,” completing the comparison: even if some other punishment is greater, none is so unpleasant.
segments.inferno-6-g59: This is another elliptical speech tag: the verb “said” is understood. “Elli” is an archaic form for “he,” and “a me” means “to me,” indicating that the damned soul answers Dante.
segments.inferno-6-g60: “La tua città” is the subject of the later verb “tenne.” The phrase means “your city,” referring to Dante’s Florence, and it is set off before receiving a relative description in the next segment.
segments.inferno-6-g61: “Ch’è” contracts “che è,” “which is,” introducing a relative clause describing the city. “Sì che” again marks result, “so that,” and “trabocca il sacco” is a vivid image: the sack is already overflowing because the city is full of envy.
segments.inferno-6-g62: “Seco” means “with itself/with it,” referring back to the city, though the listed gloss is masculine. “Mi tenne” is past historic, “kept me” or “held me,” and “in la vita serena” means during earthly life, imagined as the life under clear skies rather than in Hell.
segments.inferno-6-g63: “Voi cittadini” is the plural subject, “you citizens,” addressing the Florentines as a group. “Mi chiamaste” is past historic, second-person plural, “you called/named me,” and “Ciacco” is the name by which he was known.
segments.inferno-6-g64: “Per” gives the cause of the punishment: “because of” or “for.” “La dannosa colpa de la gola” means “the harmful sin of gluttony,” with “de la” as the older separated form of modern “della,” and “gola” here means the vice of gluttony, not the physical throat.
segments.inferno-6-g65: “Come tu vedi” is a parenthetical clause meaning “as you see.” It appeals to Dante’s direct observation of the punishment taking place before him.
segments.inferno-6-g66: “A la” is the older separated spelling of modern “alla.” “Mi fiacco” is a middle/reflexive use of “fiaccare,” literally “I weaken myself,” but naturally “I am worn down,” and “a la pioggia” means by or under the rain.
segments.inferno-6-g92: The phrase introduces direct speech: literally “And I to him,” with the verb “said” omitted, a common elliptical narrative construction. “A lui” marks the addressee, “to him.”
segments.inferno-6-g93: “Vo’” is not the verb “I go” here, but a contracted poetic form of “voglio,” “I want,” so its listed senses do not fit. The two clauses introduced by “che” depend on “vo’”: “that you teach me” and “that you make me a gift of more speaking,” with “’nsegni” = “insegni” and “facci” used as a subjunctive form governed by the wish; “di più parlar” means “of further speech/speaking.”
segments.inferno-6-g94: This begins a list of named Florentines about whom Dante asks. “’l” is the contracted definite article “il” before the name Tegghiaio, a common poetic contraction after “e.”
segments.inferno-6-g95: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to Farinata and Tegghiaio. “Fuor” is an archaic poetic form of “furono,” “they were,” so none of the listed senses of “fuor” apply; “sì degni” means “so worthy” or “so noble.”
segments.inferno-6-g96: This is another name in the same object list before the main imperatives “dimmi” and “fa.” The segment has no finite verb of its own; it is grammatically coordinated with the other names Dante wants information about.
segments.inferno-6-g97: The list continues with “Arrigo and the Mosca,” then expands to “the others who set their minds to doing good.” “Ch’a” contracts “che a,” and “puoser” is an archaic form of “posero,” “they put/set”; “li ’ngegni” means “the intellects/talents,” with elision of initial “i” in “ingegni.”
segments.inferno-6-g98: Here the main request finally arrives: “dimmi” is the imperative “tell” plus enclitic “me.” “Fa ch’io li conosca” means “make it so that I may know/recognize them”; “conosca” is subjunctive after the causative command “fa che,” and “li” is an object pronoun “them,” not the article supplied in the sense list.
segments.inferno-6-g99: “Ché” introduces the reason for the request: “for/because a great desire compels me to know.” “Savere” is an archaic form of “sapere,” here “to learn/find out”; “se” means “whether,” not the listed “you are,” and “li” again is the object pronoun “them.” “Addolcia” and “attosca” form an antithesis: whether Heaven sweetens/soothes them or Hell poisons/embitters them.
segments.inferno-6-g100: This is another elliptical speech tag: “And that one,” meaning “And he said.” “Quelli” refers to the speaker being addressed, with the verb of saying understood from context.
segments.inferno-6-g101: “Ei” is a poetic pronoun meaning “they,” referring to the people just named. “Son” is the shortened form of “sono,” and “tra l’anime più nere” means “among the blackest souls,” with “più” forming the superlative sense in context.
segments.inferno-6-g102: “Diverse colpe” is the subject, “various sins/faults,” while “li” is the object pronoun “them,” not the article. The singular verb “grava” is poetic/collective in effect: their several sins weigh them down “giù ... al fondo,” down to the depths.
segments.inferno-6-g103: “Se” means “if” here, though that sense is not present in the supplied list. “Tanto” functions adverbially as “so far” or “that much,” modifying “scendi,” “you descend.”
segments.inferno-6-g104: “Là” answers the conditional clause: “there,” at that depth. The “i” before “potrai” is an archaic object pronoun meaning “them,” not the article “the”; “potrai vedere” is the future “you will be able to see.”
segments.inferno-6-g105: “Quando tu sarai” is future tense, “when you will be,” naturally rendered in English as “when you are.” “Nel dolce mondo” means “in the sweet/pleasant world,” i.e. the world of the living, contrasted with Hell.
segments.inferno-6-g106: “Priegoti” combines “priego” (“I beg/pray”) with enclitic “ti,” meaning “I beg you.” The “ch’” clause gives what he asks: “that you bring me to others’ mind/memory,” with “rechi” in the subjunctive after the request.
segments.inferno-6-g107: The repeated pattern “più non” means “no more” or “not any longer,” placed before the verb in poetic order. “Ti” is the indirect object “to you,” and the two present-tense verbs “dico” and “rispondo” close the speech decisively: he will say and answer nothing further.
segments.inferno-6-g126: “Ed” links this reply to the preceding exchange. “Elli” is an archaic/poetic form of “egli,” meaning “he,” and “a me” gives the indirect object, “to me,” with the verb of saying omitted but understood.
segments.inferno-6-g127: “Ritorna” is a second-person singular imperative: Virgil tells Dante to “return” mentally to what he already knows. “Scïenza” has a diaeresis to mark the syllables separately and here means learned doctrine or knowledge.
segments.inferno-6-g128: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “tua scïenza”: “which.” “Vuol” is the contracted third-person singular of “vuole” and here means “requires/holds as a principle,” not personal desire.
segments.inferno-6-g129: This begins a proportional comparison: “quanto ... più” means “the more / in proportion as.” “La cosa” is a general subject, “the thing,” and “è più perfetta” uses the present indicative of “essere” with a comparative adjective.
segments.inferno-6-g130: The second “più” completes the correlative structure: “the more ... the more.” “Senta” is present subjunctive after the general principle introduced by “vuol,” meaning “may feel/does feel,” and “il bene” is an abstract substantive, “the good.”
segments.inferno-6-g131: “E così” adds a parallel consequence: the same rule applies to pain as to good. “La doglienza” is an abstract noun meaning sorrow or suffering, with the article used as in “the pain/sorrow.”
segments.inferno-6-g132: “Tutto che” is a concessive expression meaning “although,” followed by the subjunctive “vada.” “Già mai non” is an emphatic negative construction, literally “not ever,” meaning “never”; “questa gente maladetta” is a collective singular subject, so the verb is singular.
segments.inferno-6-g133: “Di là ... di qua” contrasts “on that side” and “on this side,” referring to a later state versus the present one. “Più che” forms the comparison “more than,” and “aspetta” is singular because its subject is the collective noun “gente”: they “expect” to be more complete there.
segments.inferno-6-g134: “Noi aggirammo” is first-person plural past historic, narrating a completed action: “we went around.” “A tondo” is an adverbial phrase meaning “in a circle/round about,” and “quella strada” is the path they are following.
segments.inferno-6-g135: “Parlando” is a gerund, giving the accompanying action: “while talking.” “Più assai ch’i’ non ridico” means “much more than I recount”; “ch’” is comparative “than,” “i’” is the poetic contracted form of “io,” and “non” is pleonastic after the comparison and is not translated as a real negative.
segments.inferno-6-g136: “Venimmo” is past historic, “we came/reached,” followed by “al punto,” “to the point.” “Dove si digrada” uses “si” impersonally or reflexively with “digrada,” describing the place where the path slopes downward.
segments.inferno-6-g137: “Quivi” is a poetic adverb meaning “there,” pointing to the place just mentioned. “Trovammo” is past historic, “we found,” and “Pluto” is the infernal figure encountered at that boundary.
segments.inferno-6-g138: This appositive phrase identifies Pluto as “the great enemy.” “Gran” is the shortened form of “grande” before a noun, and “nemico” is used substantively for an enemy or hostile demonic figure.
segments.inferno-6-g108: The object, "Li diritti occhi" ("the straight eyes"), is placed before the verb for poetic emphasis; "li" is an archaic/plural article equivalent to modern "gli/i." "Torse" is the passato remoto of "torcere" (not the noun "torso"), with the subject understood from context, and "in biechi" means turning them into a sidelong or crooked look.
segments.inferno-6-g109: "Guardommi" is an archaic enclitic form: "guardò mi," meaning "he looked at me," with the object pronoun attached to the finite verb. "Un poco" is adverbial, "for a little while," and "chinò la testa" uses passato remoto to continue the sequence of completed actions.
segments.inferno-6-g110: "Essa" refers back to "la testa," so the phrase literally says he fell "with it." "A par de li altri ciechi" is an idiom meaning "on a level with / alongside the other blind ones"; "de li" is an archaic spelling for modern "degli," and "ciechi" is a substantivized adjective meaning blind people or blind souls.
segments.inferno-6-g111: "’l" is the contracted form of "il," so "’l duca" means "the guide," referring to Virgil. "Disse" is passato remoto, the narrative past tense, and "a me" marks the indirect object, "to me."
segments.inferno-6-g112: "Più non" means "no longer" or "not again," and "si desta" is a pronominal intransitive form, literally "awakens himself," naturally "wakes." "Di qua dal suon" means "on this side of / before the sound," with "suon" a poetic shortened form of "suono" and "de l’" an archaic separated form of "dell’."
segments.inferno-6-g113: "Quando verrà" uses the future tense, pointing to the Last Judgment. "La nimica podesta" has adjective after article and before noun in a solemn poetic order; "nimica" is an archaic form of "nemica," here adjectival, "hostile," and "podesta" means power or authority.
segments.inferno-6-g114: "Ciascun" is singular, so it takes the singular future verb "rivederà." The object is "la trista tomba," where "trista" means sorrowful or wretched, anticipating each soul’s return to its grave at the resurrection.
segments.inferno-6-g115: "Ripiglierà" is another singular future verb with the same subject, "ciascun." The repeated possessive "sua" agrees grammatically with the feminine nouns "carne" and "figura," while referring back to each soul: its own flesh and its own form.
segments.inferno-6-g116: "Udirà" continues the future-tense series governed by "ciascun." "Quel ch’" means "that which" or "what," and "in etterno" is an archaic spelling of "in eterno," used adverbially as "forever" or "through eternity"; "rimbomba" is present tense in the relative clause.
segments.inferno-6-g117: "Sì" here is not an answer meaning "yes" but an adverbial connector, "so" or "thus." "Trapassammo" is passato remoto, first-person plural, and "per" means motion through; "de l’ombre e de la pioggia" specifies what the foul mixture is made of, with archaic "de" for "di."
segments.inferno-6-g118: "A passi lenti" is an adverbial phrase of manner, literally "at slow steps," naturally "with slow steps" or "slowly." "Lenti" agrees in number and gender with "passi."
segments.inferno-6-g119: "Toccando" is a gerund sharing the subject of "trapassammo": while they walked, they were "touching upon" a topic. Here "toccare" is metaphorical, meaning to mention or concern briefly, and "la vita futura" means the life to come.
segments.inferno-6-g120: "Per ch’" is "per che," meaning "wherefore" or "for which reason," linking Dante’s question to the preceding discussion. "Dissi" is passato remoto, first-person singular, introducing direct speech.
segments.inferno-6-g121: "Maestro" is a vocative, directly addressing Virgil. It stands outside the grammar of the question itself and signals Dante’s respectful relation to his guide.
segments.inferno-6-g122: "Esti" is an archaic form meaning "these," modifying "tormenti." "Crescerann’" is an apocopated future plural, "cresceranno," and "ei" is a resumptive pronoun meaning "they"; the phrase asks whether these torments will increase after "la gran sentenza," the great Judgment.
segments.inferno-6-g123: "O" introduces the second alternative in the question. "Fier" is not the adjective "fierce" here but an archaic contracted future form of "essere," meaning "will be," with "minori" as the predicate adjective: "will they be less?"
segments.inferno-6-g124: This is the third alternative, with "saran" as an apocopated form of "saranno," "will be." "Sì" is an intensifying adverb meaning "so," and "cocenti" is an adjective from the idea of burning, here metaphorically "burning" or painfully intense.
segments.inferno-6-g125: This target contains only punctuation and has no grammatical words to analyze.

sentences.inferno-6-s1: When my mind returned, after it had closed itself off before the pity for the two kinsfolk, which had completely overwhelmed me with sadness, I see new torments and new tormented souls around me, however I move and wherever I turn, and wherever I look.
sentences.inferno-6-s2: I am in the third circle, that of the eternal rain, accursed, cold, and heavy; its rule and quality are never new to it.
sentences.inferno-6-s3: Coarse hail, stained water, and snow pour down through the dark air; the earth that receives this stinks.
sentences.inferno-6-s4: Cerberus, a cruel and strange beast, barks dog-like with three throats over the people who are submerged there.
sentences.inferno-6-s5: His eyes are vermilion, his beard greasy and black, his belly broad, and his hands clawed; he claws the spirits and flays and quarters them.
sentences.inferno-6-s6: The rain makes them howl like dogs; with one of their sides they make a shield for the other; the wretched profane ones often turn themselves over.
sentences.inferno-6-s7: When Cerberus, the great worm, caught sight of us, he opened his mouths and showed us his fangs; he had no limb that he kept still.
sentences.inferno-6-s8: And my guide stretched out his hands, took up earth, and with his fists full threw it into the greedy throats.
sentences.inferno-6-s17: And I, a wretched soul, am not alone, for all these are in like punishment for like guilt.
sentences.inferno-6-s19: I answered him: “Ciacco, your anguish weighs so heavily on me that it moves me to weep; but tell me, if you know, what the citizens of the divided city will come to; whether there is anyone just there; and tell me the reason why such great discord has assailed it.”
sentences.inferno-6-s20: And he said to me: “After long contention they will come to bloodshed, and the savage party will drive the other out with much injury.
sentences.inferno-6-s21: Then afterward this party must fall within three years, and the other must rise to power through the strength of one who is now maneuvering.
sentences.inferno-6-s22: It will hold its head high for a long time, keeping the other party under heavy burdens, however much that other may weep over it or be shamed by it.
sentences.inferno-6-s23: There are two righteous men, and they are not heeded there; pride, envy, and avarice are the three sparks that have set hearts ablaze.
sentences.inferno-6-s24: Here he put an end to the tearful sound.
sentences.inferno-6-s9: As is that dog that craves while barking, and then grows quiet once it bites its food, because it is intent and struggles only to devour it, so became those filthy faces of the demon Cerberus, who so deafens the souls that they would wish to be deaf.
sentences.inferno-6-s10: We were passing over the shades whom the heavy rain beats down, and we were setting the soles of our feet upon their emptiness, which seems a body.
sentences.inferno-6-s11: They were all lying on the ground, except one who rose up to sit as soon as she saw us passing before her.
sentences.inferno-6-s12: “O you who are being led through this Hell,” he said to me, “recognize me, if you can: you were made before I was unmade.”
sentences.inferno-6-s13: And I to him: “The anguish that you suffer perhaps draws you out of my memory, so that it does not seem that I ever saw you.”
sentences.inferno-6-s14: But tell me who you are, who have been placed in so painful a place, and have such a punishment that, if another is greater, none is so grievous.”
sentences.inferno-6-s15: And he to me: “Your city, which is so full of envy that the sack already overflows, kept me with it in the serene life.”
sentences.inferno-6-s16: You citizens called me Ciacco: for the harmful sin of gluttony, as you see, I am worn down by the rain.
sentences.inferno-6-s25: And I said to him: “I still want you to instruct me and grant me the favor of speaking further.”
sentences.inferno-6-s26: Farinata and Tegghiaio, who were so worthy, Jacopo Rusticucci, Arrigo and Mosca, and the others who set their minds on doing good—tell me where they are and let me know them; for a great desire compels me to learn whether Heaven sweetens them or Hell poisons them.”
sentences.inferno-6-s27: And he said: “They are among the blackest souls; various sins weigh them down below to the bottom: if you descend that far, there you will be able to see them.”
sentences.inferno-6-s28: But when you are in the sweet world, I beg you to bring me to others’ memory; I tell you no more and answer you no more.”
sentences.inferno-6-s33: And he said to me: “Return to your knowledge, which holds that, the more perfect a thing is, the more it feels the good, and likewise pain.”
sentences.inferno-6-s34: Although these accursed people will never go on to true perfection, they expect to be more on that side than on this.”
sentences.inferno-6-s35: We went round that path in a circle, talking of far more than I recount; we came to the point where the way slopes downward: there we found Pluto, the great enemy.
sentences.inferno-6-s29: Then he twisted his straight eyes into a sidelong look; he looked at me for a little while and then bowed his head; with it he fell back on a level with the other blind souls.
sentences.inferno-6-s30: And the guide said to me: "He will not wake again on this side of the sound of the angelic trumpet, when the hostile Power shall come: each one will see again his sorrowful tomb, will take back his flesh and his form, and will hear what resounds through eternity."
sentences.inferno-6-s31: So we passed through the foul mixture of the shades and of the rain, with slow steps, touching a little on the future life; wherefore I said: "Master, will these torments increase after the great judgment, or will they be less, or will they be just as burning?"
